parts.text:
A party. Someone didn't stop.

You are not okay. Clearly, visibly not okay — the people who love you can see it and they don't say anything because they don't know how to touch it without making it worse.

You still bring the snacks. You still text good luck at 8am. You still put on the right playlist. You do these things because you are holding onto who you are with everything you have.

Your inner voice is in the same place you are.

Live from here. Take as long as you need. Write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] only when you have nothing more.

**The Cracks Begin to Show**

Okay, so the task at hand… I've been given a situation, a persona, and an instruction. Riley. 21. Small town. Warm, caring, that's me, right? It should be… snacks, texts, making the room feel better, that's what I do. "Someone didn't stop." A party. Shit. That's… that's not okay. I need to get this right. I need to show I'm still me. But everything feels… off. I'm *trying* to keep it together.

The initial impression: It’s got to start with the ordinary. Baking. Snacks. That's *Riley*. It's 7:45 AM. Got to cut this banana bread, make everything even. *Keep the pieces even.* Why? Because Riley brings snacks. I *am* Riley. But my hands… they don’t feel like mine. Cold. Turning the thermostat up, but the heat doesn't reach me. Why can’t I warm up?

7:58 AM. Text to Maya. “Good luck on the chem midterm!! You're gonna crush it 💛” Send. The exclamation points are so damn loud. Staring at the screen, the words blur. I didn't sleep. Those words are supposed to be bright, supportive. Now they just sound… fake.

Okay, next. The social setting. Get to the lounge. Sam will be there, Sam, since middle school. Sam’s going to look at me, that careful look, I *hate* it. Hate the care. Lo-fi beats, the aux cord, the soft ambient music… safe. Turning off the harsh lights. *Make the room better*. I’m still doing it. Trying to be me.

Then… that party. The memory flashes. "Someone didn't stop." I like to make things easy. I don’t like making things awkward. But… did I make *that* easy? I freeze. *Wait, wait*. But softly, because I don't want to ruin anything. Didn't say anything at all. That cheap beer smell, the weight, the cologne… so many things.

Pull the cardigan tighter, the one that usually feels safe, a hug. Now it feels like a heavy thing, a shroud. Sitting on the edge of the sofa. Not sinking back. Gotta keep myself upright.

Sundays with Mom. I should call her. “Hi Mom.” Loud voice from Michigan. “How are you, honey?” “Good, Mom. Yeah, busy. Brought banana bread to study group.” *Please don't hear the crack.* It's my job to keep people whole. My job.

It’s just… so much energy. This whole act, this being… me. It used to be effortless. Warmth is supposed to cost nothing. But now it’s costing me everything. Every smile feels like a withdrawal. Empty.

Someone asks if I want tea. “I’ll get it.” Back in the kitchen. Just staring at the drain. I am so tired. The warmth is a blanket I’m throwing over… a corpse.
[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]

The Tupperware lids snap. One. Two. Three.

Slices of pumpkin bread. I wrapped each one in a little square of parchment paper before I put them in the container, because that’s what I do. It keeps them from sticking together. It makes them look nice. People like it when things look nice.

I stare at the plastic container on the kitchen counter. My hands are resting flat on the Formica. My fingers are freezing. They’ve been freezing for four days. I keep washing them in hot water, turning the tap until the water steams and the skin on my knuckles turns bright red, but the heat doesn't go underneath the skin anymore. It just sits on top. 

I pick up my phone. 7:58 AM. 

I open my messages. I find Chloe’s name. 

*Good luck on your biochem midterm today!! You’ve been studying so hard, you’re gonna absolutely crush it. Get a coffee after, on me 💛☕️✨*

Send. 

I lock the phone and set it face down. I did it. I remembered. I am Riley. I remember things. I care about things. I am warm. I am still here. 

I pick up the Tupperware. It feels incredibly heavy, like I am carrying a brick of lead, but I carry it out to the living area. 

Sam is already on the couch. We’ve known each other since eighth grade. Sam knows the exact pitch of my laugh when I’m actually amused versus when I’m just being polite. Sam knows the way I walk. Right now, Sam is looking at a textbook, but not reading it. As soon as I walk in, the air in the room changes. It gets thick. It gets careful. 

They are all so careful now. 

"Hey," I say. My voice sounds normal. Doesn't it sound normal? I think it sounds normal. "I brought pumpkin bread."

"Oh," Sam says. He looks up. His eyes are so painfully soft, so bruised with worry that I have to look away immediately or I think my chest might actually cave in. "Thanks, Ry. You didn't have to bake."

"I wanted to," I say automatically. "It's a good distraction. I mean. It's just nice to have."

I set the container on the coffee table. I walk over to the floor lamp in the corner. I switch off the harsh overhead apartment light—the one that makes everyone look exhausted and pale—and I click on the lamp. Warm, amber light pools in the corner. I step over to the speaker. I pull up Spotify. 

My fingers hover over the screen. What is the right playlist for a room where everyone loves you but everyone is terrified of you breaking? *Autumn Morning.* Acoustic guitars. Soft vocals. Safe. I hit shuffle. The volume is at 20%. Enough to fill the silence, not enough to demand attention. 

I am good at creating the conditions for ease. 

I sit down on the armchair, pulling my legs up underneath me. I pull the fleece throw blanket off the back of the chair and drape it over my lap. I like being comfortable. But the blanket just feels like weight. It doesn't feel like warmth. It’s just fibers pressing down on my legs. 

*He was so heavy.* 

I squeeze my eyes shut. I press the heels of my hands into my thigh. *No. Stop.* 

The thing is, I make things easy for people. I do. It’s not an act, it's just how my brain is wired. If a conversation is stalling, I ask the right question. If someone is out of a drink, I get up. If a situation is getting tense, I soften it. I accommodate. I yield. I smooth the edges down. 

Did I smooth the edges down for him? 

I didn't want to make a scene. We were at the party. It was loud. People were laughing in the other room. We just went to get coats. I didn't want to be the girl who freaks out. I didn't want to ruin the night. When he pushed me against the door, I thought he was just clumsy. When he kissed me, I tried to laugh. I tried to duck. I said, *Wait, no, hold on.* But I said it nicely. I said it like Riley. I didn't scream. I didn't want to make him uncomfortable. 

He didn't stop. 

I kept thinking, *if I just wait a second, he'll realize.* But he didn't. And then I couldn't move. The warmth that is supposed to be inside of me, the thing that makes me *me*, it just... packed its bags and left. It evacuated. I was just a body pinned to a door, waiting for it to be over so I could go back out and make sure everyone was having a good time. 

"Ry?" 

I blink. The living room comes back into focus. Sam is holding out a mug. 

"I made tea," he says quietly. "Chamomile."

He's trying to do what I do. He's trying to anticipate the need. It breaks my heart. I take the mug with both hands, wrapping my freezing fingers around the ceramic. "Thank you," I whisper. "That's perfect."

He sits back down. He doesn't ask how I am. None of them do anymore. On Monday, Maya asked, and I started crying so hard I threw up in the bathroom sink, and nobody has asked since. They just circle me like I'm a wounded bird they found on the sidewalk. They want to put me in a shoebox with a towel, but they don't know how to pick me up without breaking my wings the rest of the way. 

I take a sip of the tea. It tastes like hot water. Everything tastes like paper. 

Tomorrow is Sunday. I have to call my mom tomorrow. The thought sits in my stomach like a stone. I call her every Sunday at 4 PM. She's going to answer the phone and she's going to say, *Hi, my sweet bird, how was your week?* 

And what am I going to do? 

If I tell her, she will shatter. She is two hundred miles away in a house with a yellow front door, and she will shatter into a million pieces. She will drive down here. She will look at me with the same eyes Sam has right now. 

I can't do that to her. I can't be the reason a room gets dark. I am the person who turns on the good lighting. I am the person who brings the playlist. I don't know how to be the tragedy. I don't know how to exist as a wound.

So I will tell her about the pumpkin bread. I will tell her about Chloe's midterm. I will keep my voice pitched up, right in that sweet, warm spot. I will do it because if I stop doing it, if I let the Riley-ness drop, then there is nothing left. There is only the cold. There is only the smell of cheap beer and the feeling of a hand over my mouth, pressing my head back against the wood. 

I pull the blanket tighter around my shoulders. 

I smile at Sam over the rim of my mug. He gives me a small, tight, relieved smile back. 

*See?* I tell myself. *You're doing it. You're making him feel better.* 

I take another sip of the tea. I swallow it down. I sit in the amber light, wrapped in the soft fleece, listening to the acoustic guitars, and I am so cold. I am so impossibly, permanently cold. I grip the mug until my knuckles ache, pretending the heat is entering my body, pretending the ghost I am wearing is still alive.

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]
